coredata.dc:title: Deep processing of Pluronics: Production of surface-active carbon dots and application in photoluminescent soft materials
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Ning
Li, Shulin
Liu, Xiaoqian
Ji, Xiaoli
Feng, Lei
Li, Hongguang
coredata.dc:description: Poly(ethylene oxide)-poly(propylene oxide)-poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO-PPO-PEO) triblock copolymers, with trademark of Pluronics, are an important class of nonionic, macromolecular surfactants. In this work, deep processing of Pluronics was tried through pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) in Pluronics, leading to the formation of blue-emitting, surface-active carbon dots (sa-CDs) where the PEO-PPO-PEO chains are covalently attached to the carbonized CA. Production of sa-CDs is successful for all three Pluronics including F127, F68 and P123. The sa-CDs could be produced facilely and massively. Specifically in the case of F127, the surface activity becomes stronger after deep processing. The sa-CDs were used as the photoluminescent component to construct microemulsions using liquid flavor or essential oils extracted from plants as the oil phase. The controlled release of the liquid flavor or essential oil in the microemulsions provides opportunities of formula modulation towards applications in food and cosmetics. By further introducing chiral molecules to the system, circularly polarized luminescence was achieved, which opens the door for applications in photonic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface active
Carbon dots
Pluronic
Microemulsion
Circularly polarized luminescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875129), and the assistance of Center for Structural and Property Analysis, Core Facilities Sharing Platform, Shandong University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
a) Statistics of the PL intensity of F127-CDs obtained at different W CA/F127. Inset is a photo under 365 nm UV irradiation of the sample with the highest PL intensity. b) TGA curves for F127-CDs, F127 and CA. Where T
d means the temperature at which the derivative weight < −0.05. c) FTIR spectra for F127-CDs, F127 and CA. d) DSC curves of solid F127-CDs and F127. High resolution XPS energy spectra of C 1s (e) and O 1s (f) for F127-CDs. g-i) HRTEM images of F127-CDs (prepared from an extremely dilute aqueous solution of 0.005 mg mL−1) and corresponding size distribution of the carbon cores (statistics on 51 individuals). The CDs used in Fig. 1a were as-prepared, while others are purified through dialysis.

a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra at varying λ ex for F127-CDs in water (0.9 mg mL−1). The absorption of F127 with the same concentration was also given for comparison. b) Stokes shift and maximum PL intensity for F127-CDs in different solvents (0.9 mg mL−1, at the optimized excitation), the polarity parameter (ET N) of each solvent is given on the horizontal axis. c) Variation of the surface tension and integrated PL intensity as a function of concentration for F127-CDs. Surface tension of F127 was also given for comparison. d) Dynamic surface tension for F127 and F127-CDs in water (3 mg mL−1). The inset shows the fit to the DST data using the Rosen model, where K=(γ0-γt)/(γt-γeq). e) Particle size distribution based on DLS for F127-CDs in water with different concentrations. f) A typical cryo-TEM image for aqueous solution of F127-CDs at 0.9 mg mL−1.

a) Semi-log pots of surface tension for F127-CDs aqueous solutions as a function of concentration under different temperatures. b) Statistics on the cmc of F127-CDs at different temperatures. c) Statistics of the surface tension of F127-CDs in water at different temperatures at a concentration of 5 mg mL−1 d, e) Fluorescence spectra and statistics of the PL intensity of 0.9 mg mL−1 F127-CDs in water recorded at different temperatures. f) DLS measurements on 0.9 mg mL−1 F127-CDs in water at different temperatures.

a) Illustration of the three selected Pluronics showing the rough composition of PPO (the red part) and PEO (the blue part) chains. b) FTIR spectra of sa-CDs prepared by different Pluronics. c) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum for sa-CDs in water (0.9 mg mL−1, λ
ex = 365 nm for F127-CDs, 2 mg mL−1, λ
ex = 380 nm for F68-CDs and 2 mg mL−1, λ
ex = 340 nm for P123-CDs). d) Particle size distribution from DLS for 5 mg mL−1 sa-CDs aqueous solution. e) Semi-log-plot of surface tension of sa-CDs aqueous solution with different concentrations at 25 °C. f) DST for sa-CDs aqueous solution (3 mg mL−1, 25 °C). Data from F127-CDs were provided for comparison. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

a) Structures of the key components used to prepare MEs. b-e) Photos (b, c) taken under room light (top row) and 365 nm UV irradiation (bottom row) and emission (d, e) of the MEs with increasing amounts of F127-CDs (b, d) and LAE (c, e), respectively. The MEs were prepared in mixed solvent of EtOH/H2O (volume ratio: 1:3). Other components involved in the MEs have been shown insets of d, e. f, g) Cryo-SEM image and statistics on the sizes of the droplets of a typical ME with 2v/v% LAE, 0.2 wt% F127-CDs, 2 wt% NaDC and 10 wt% P123, prepared in mixed solvent of EtOH/H2O (volume ratio: 1:3).

a) Absorption and CD spectra of the ME (2v/v% LAE, 0.2 wt% F127-CDs, 2 wt% NaDC and 10 wt% P123) with different amount of L-PGA (dashed lines) and D-PGA(solid lines). b) CPL spectra of the ME with 4 mg mL−1 PGA, excited at 385 nm.

head.ce:title: Deep processing of Pluronics: Production of surface-active carbon dots and application in photoluminescent soft materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, Shandong, 250100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, Shandong, 250100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poly(ethylene oxide)-poly(propylene oxide)-poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO-PPO-PEO) triblock copolymers, with trademark of Pluronics, are an important class of nonionic, macromolecular surfactants. In this work, deep processing of Pluronics was tried through pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) in Pluronics, leading to the formation of blue-emitting, surface-active carbon dots (sa-CDs) where the PEO-PPO-PEO chains are covalently attached to the carbonized CA. Production of sa-CDs is successful for all three Pluronics including F127, F68 and P123. The sa-CDs could be produced facilely and massively. Specifically in the case of F127, the surface activity becomes stronger after deep processing. The sa-CDs were used as the photoluminescent component to construct microemulsions using liquid flavor or essential oils extracted from plants as the oil phase. The controlled release of the liquid flavor or essential oil in the microemulsions provides opportunities of formula modulation towards applications in food and cosmetics. By further introducing chiral molecules to the system, circularly polarized luminescence was achieved, which opens the door for applications in photonic devices.

Surface active carbon dots (sa-CDs) possessing excellent photoluminescence (PL) and surface activity was prepared by deep processing of Pluronic. One of the sa-CDs, F127-CDs, was carefully investigated and applied to prepare microemulsion with PL properties. By regulating the interface composition of microemulsion, the CPL emission of microemulsion can be activated.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The light of carbon dots (CDs) shines more and more brilliantly in the last twenty years [1]. At present, CDs have been widely used in fields such as biology [2,3], optics [4,5], energy [6,7], and chemistry [8]. The diverse functions come from the rich and adjustable structure of CDs [9]. Initially, CDs were obtained from the manipulation of bulk carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes [10], graphite-based mixture [11] and candle soot [12], which produced CDs covered by hydrophilic groups such as carboxy and hydroxy. With the fast progress of CDs production by pyrolysis, abundant organic molecules and their mixtures were applied as the starting materials, which significantly expand the family of CDs [13,14]. Typically, CDs modified with alkyl or siloxane chains represent a new class of branched nanostructures connected at the central carbonized core [15,16]. If the peripheral chains are long-chain polymers, hairy CDs would be obtained [17].

Unlike the highly hydrophilic CDs covered by carboxy and hydroxy, the enrichment of the surface modifying groups of CDs may impart them amphiphilicity. Amphiphilic CDs were reported early in 2012 [18]. However, the concept proposed in this case and many subsequent literatures is restricted to the varied solubilities of CDs in different solvents [19–22]. The CDs with explicit surface activity (denoted as sa-CDs hereafter) proved by surface tension measurements were reported only recently (Table S1) [23–29]. The appearance of sa-CDs built a bridge between photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterials and surfactants, avoiding the complicated and tedious synthetic procedures involved in the production of traditional PL surfactants. In addition, the capability of CDs against photobleaching gives better photostability of sa-CDs over traditional PL surfactants [30,31].

Being a relatively new branch of CDs, the development of sa-CDs is still in its infancy. From a viewpoint of preparation, sa-CDs were obtained mainly by chemical modification on the pre-formed CDs [23–26]. One-pot pyrolysis, which is more time-saving than post-functionalization, has been rarely tried [25,27]. From a viewpoint of the structure, the majority of the reported sa-CDs are functionalized with alkyl chains [23–26,28], which suffered from poor solubility in water.

Recently, we demonstrated that by pyrolyzing citric acid (CA) in triethylene glycol monomethyl ether (TGME), esterification between the carboxy of CA and the terminal hydroxy of TGME could occur, leading to the formation of sa-CDs covered by oligoxyethylene groups which showed high water solubility [27]. Poly(ethylene oxide)-poly(propylene oxide)-poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO-PPO-PEO) triblock copolymers are an important class of macromolecular surfactants with trademark of Pluronics [32]. Triggered by our initial discovery [27], in this work we report the pyrolysis of CA in three Pluronics (F127, P123, F68), leading to the formation of sa-CDs. The performance of the sa-CDs is better than the TGME-derived CDs recently reported by our group, and surpasses the pristine Pluronic in case of F127. As a typical example of application, the sa-CDs were used to construct PL microemulsions (ME) using liquid flavor (linalyl acetate, LAE) or essential oils extracted from different plants as the oil phase. Compared to the bulk solution, the release of the liquid flavor or essential oil becomes smooth and slower, providing opportunities of formula modulation towards applications in food and cosmetics. By introducing chiral molecules (phenylglycine alcohol, PGA) to the interfacial films, circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) was achieved, which opens the door for applications of the PLME in photonic devices.

The organic solvents, including N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), toluene, ethanol (EtOH), and trichloromethane (TCM) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA), sodium deoxycholate (NaDC), Pluronic P123 (P123, (EO)20(PO)70(EO)20), Pluronic F68 (F68, (EO)75(PO)30(EO)75) and linalyl acetate (LAE) were obtained from Sigma. Pluronic F127 (F127, (EO)99(PO)67(EO)99) was bought from BASF (China) Co., Ltd. Phenylglycine alcohol (PGA), including R-PGA and L-PGA, R-2-phenyl-1-propanol (R–PP) were bought from Sigma. All the chemicals were used without further purification. Ultrapure water used in the experiments with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm was obtained using a UPH-IV ultrapure water purifier (China).

F127-CDs were obtained by solvent-free pyrolysis. In a typical experiment, 15 g of F127 and 10 g of CA were mixed and heated in an oil bath under stirring. When the temperature of the oil bath reached 195 °C, timing was started and pyrolysis was continued at ∼200 °C. After 2 h, heating was stopped and stirring was continued. After the temperature dropped below 100 °C, 1 L of ultrapure water was added under stirring. After the temperature reached room temperature, the suspension was transferred into dialysis bags (cutting off molecular weight: 14,000 Da) and dialyzed against water in a beaker with a volume of 8 L. The water outside the dialysis bags was replaced every 36 h (at least 7 times). After dialysis, the suspension was filtered and lyophilized.

Prior to the formulation of microemulsions, a series of mother liquor were prepared including 40 wt% P123 in EtOH, 2 wt% aqueous solution of F127-CDs, and 10 wt% NaDC aqueous solution. In a typical experiment, 0.25 mL of 40 wt% P123 ethanol solution, 0.1 mL of 2 wt% F127-CDs aqueous solution and 0.2 mL of 10 wt% NaDC aqueous solution were mixed, to which 0.02 mL of LAE was added. The mixture was supplemented with ultrapure water to make a total volume of 1 mL. After vigorous shaking, the mixture was kept in a 25 °C thermostat for further characterization. For microemulsions containing phenylglycine alcohol (PGA), D-PGA or L-PGA was added to the 40 wt% EtOH solution of P123 to prepare a mixed solution with 10 mg mL−1 PGA. For the microemulsion containing R–PP, LAE/R–PP (1/1, v/v) was used as the oil phase.

The images of F127-CDs aggregates were observed by cryo-TEM. The sample was prepared in the Cryoplunge TM3 (Cp3, Gatan, USA) at ambient temperature. Wherein, the sample was then rapidly frozen by liquid ethane (∼-165 °C, cooled by liquid nitrogen). The frozen sample was transferred to a liquid nitrogen-cooled Gatan 626 sample rod and finally entered into a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-1400) for observation, operating at 120 kV. The cold trap of the transmission electron microscope was maintained at −174 °C by adding liquid nitrogen. Sample images were recorded by a Gatan 831 CCD (2.7K × 2.7K). For cryo-SEM, the sample preparation process was performed by adding a drop of sample (∼0.02 mL) to the sample table, placing the sample table in the cryostat workstation EM VCM (Leica, Germany), freezing rapidly under liquid nitrogen and transferring the sample table via the cryotransfer attachment EM VCT500 (Leica, Germany) to the high vacuum cryo-coater EM ACE600 (Leica, Germany) for sublimation at −95 °C for 10 min, followed by cooling to −150 °C for platinum spraying and subsequent transfer to the SEM (Zeiss GeminiSEM 500) for observation via the Cryotransfer Attachment EM VCT500 (Leica, Germany), where the SEM cold trap is maintained at −180 °C by adding liquid nitrogen. Monodisperse F127-CDs were observed using Talos F200X high-resolution field emission transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Thermo Fisher, USA). The indicated concentrations of F127-CDs solutions were added dropwise to the copper grids and observed after baking for 35 min using an infrared lamp.

Confocal laser scan microscopy (CLSM) observations were performed using an LabRAM HR Evolution inverted microscope (HORIBA, French) equipped with a high-numerical-aperture 60 oil-immersed objective lens. The temperature was maintained at 25.0 ± 0.1 °C with the help of a cyclic water bath. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra in KBr pellets were recorded on a VERTEX-70/70v spectrophotometer. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was operated on ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) with a monochromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.71 eV). Small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) measurements were performed using an Anton-Paar SAX Sess mc2 system (Austria) with Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation (1.54 Å) operating at 50 kV and 40 mA.

The zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out using the Nano ZS (Malvern, UK) instrument with designated cells, all samples were filtered through a 2.2 μm PTFE membrane before measurements. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on TGA-5500 high precision thermal gravimetric analyzer with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under N2 atmosphere (TA, America). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were carried on a DSCQ20 (TA, America) with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.

The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Hitachi UV–vis 4100 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence measurements were measured on a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). The fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yield (QY) were obtained from an Edinburgh Instruments (UK) FLS920 luminescence spectrometer (xenon lamp, 450 W). The CD and CPL data were recorded on a Chirascan V100 circular dichroism spectrometer (Applied Photophysics, British). The vessel used for CD measurements was a quartz cuvette with an optical range of 0.5 mm, while CPL measurements were made with a 1 mm quadruple pass quartz cuvette.

The steady-state surface tension (SST) between water and air was measured in a Sigma 700 surface tension meter (Biolin Scientific, Germany) with plate method. The surface tension was measured three times with a 90 s interval between each measurement. The dynamic surface tension (DST) between water and air was measured using the bubble pressure method in a BP100 surface tension meter (KRŰSS Company, Germany). Before the measurement, the surface tension of water was ascertained in the range of 72.0 ± 0.5 mN m−1.

The real time release of LAE from PLME was monitored with a combined system (STA-FTIR GC/MS, PerkinElmer, TGA8000 + Spectrum Two + ClarusS, USA) starting from synchronous thermal analysis which ensures that the temperature of micro emulsion is stabilized at 20 or 50 °C. The evaporated ingredients were analyzed by gas chromatography (GC).
All the other characterizations were carried out at 25.0 °C unless other stated.

The sa-CDs were prepared in solvent free state by pyrolyzing a mixture of CA and Pluronic at 200 °C for 2 h, with F127, a widely-studied Pluronic, being selected first (Fig. S1). After pyrolysis, luminescent products with similar surface tension (Fig. S2a) were obtained under 365 nm UV irradiation when dispersed in water, with the strongest emission occurring at an optimized CA/F127 wt ratio (W CA/F127) of 2:3 (Fig. 1 a, Figs. S2b–d). The sa-CDs obtained at this W CA/F127 (denoted as F127-CDs hereafter) were then purified by dialysis and subjected to detailed investigation. Being a popular raw material for CDs, CA is known to undergo effective carbonization above 180 °C [33,34], which is also confirmed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) given in Fig. 1b where obvious weight loss of CA starts at ∼166 °C. For F127, however, no obvious weight loss was noticed before 200 °C (Fig. 1b). Thus, for F127, only condensation of the terminal hydroxy groups could be expected during pyrolysis, leading to covalent connection of the PEO-PPO-PEO chain to the carbonized core. This assumption gains proofs from structural analysis on the F127-CDs. From Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 1c), F127-CDs share characteristic peaks with F127, including the C–H stretching vibration at 2900 cm−1 and C–O stretching vibration at 1250 cm−1 [35]. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) measurements gave scattering from crystalized PEO-PPO-PEO chains for both F127-CDs and F127 (Fig. S3) [36]. Meanwhile, a signal from the CO in the ester bond was observed at 1730 cm−1 from FTIR confirming the covalent linkage of F127 to the carbonized cores [27]. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements (Fig. 1d) indicated that the temperature range of the phase transition for F127-CDs became broad with a slight shift towards low temperatures (12.3 °C for the endothermic peaks and 10.7 °C for exothermic ones), which is a typical feature for the nonlinear molecules compared to linear ones [37]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed two peaks at 533 eV and 286 eV, which were assigned to O1s and C1s, respectively (Fig. S4). The high-resolution C1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three ones at 288.87 eV, 286.06 eV and 284.55 eV, which were assigned to CO, C–O and C–C/CC, respectively (Fig. 1e). The high-resolution O1s spectrum mainly contained C–O signal at 532.64 eV together with a small one at 533.73 eV assigned to CO (Fig. 1f) [38], indicating F127-CDs only contains a small proportion of the ester bonds and the majority components are the long, peripheral PEO-PPO-PEO chains.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) observations on a dilute aqueous solution of F127-CDs (down to 0.005 mg mL−1) showed the presence of monodispersed, spherical particles with average diameter (<d>) of ∼1.6 nm (Fig. 1g and h). Considering that the F127 chains are highly hydrated, they are hardly visible under TEM. These particles are thus expected to the carbonized cores. Consistent with this assumption, from magnified image shown in Fig. 1i, uniformly oriented graphite-phase lattice stripes (∼0.21 nm) were observed [39]. As F127 has two terminal hydroxy groups which can react with the carboxy groups of CA, the exact architecture of the CDs is unknown at the moment. Two possible conformations could be envisaged, i.e., cross-linked PEO-PPO-PEO networks with the carbonized cores as the nodes, and hairy particles with only one side of the polymer attached to the carbonized core. We suppose these two possibilities might coexist in F127-CDs.

Compared with F127, the UV–vis absorbance of F127-CDs was significantly enhanced, with the absorbance tail extending to 500 nm (Fig. 2 a). The shoulder peaks at 270–310 nm and 310–400 nm originate from n-π∗ transition and π-π∗ transition, respectively [40,41]. As already seen from the as-prepared sample, F127-CDs exhibits blue emission under 365 nm UV (inset of Fig. 1a). Further investigation was carried out in aqueous solution as a function of excitation wavelength (λ ex) from 300 nm to 420 nm. As shown in Fig. 2a, the PL intensity of F127-CDs initially increases with increasing λ ex, with a maximum at λ ex = 365 nm. The position of the emission remains almost unchanged, which locates around 441 nm. After the maximum, the PL intensity decreases with increasing λ ex accompanied with an obvious red shift of the emission up to 525 nm. F127-CDs exhibited excellent solubility in solvents with different polarity, including EtOH, DMF, TCM and toluene. Investigations on PL properties were carried out within a λ ex range of 300–400 nm, where the maximum emission occurs at λ ex = 380 ± 10 nm (Fig. S5). Statistics on the stokes shift of the maximum emission and PL intensity showed that the influence of solvent molecules with F127-CDs is complicated (Fig. 2b). For example, EtOH exhibits the lowest PL intensity, but the largest stokes shift. No monotonous relationship between key PL parameters and solvent polarity could be found. Concentration-dependent measurements gave the optimal concentration of 0.9 mg mL−1 for F127-CDs at which the PL intensity reaches the maximum (Fig. 2c, Fig. S6). The lifetime of the F127-CDs was measured using water and TCM as two typical solvents. Double-exponential decay was observed (Fig. S7), with average lifetime (<τ>) to be 2.98 ns in water and 2.13 ns in TCM, which indicate that the emission is fluorescence in nature. The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) is determined to be 2.36 % in water, which is slightly higher than that of TGME-CDs. In TCM, Φ increases to 3.87 %, which is consistent with its highest PL intensity (see Fig. 2b). For most hydrophilic CDs, aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effect could be observed. In contrast, F127-CDs shows bright photoluminescence in solvent free state (Fig. S8). The capability of F127-CDs to resist the ACQ effect [42] should be ascribed to the PEO-PPO-PEO chain which well separate the carbonized cores.

As another important feature of sa-CDs, the surface tension of F127-CDs was investigated in detail. The semi-log plot of the surface tension vs concentration showed that both F127 and F127-CDs have two inflection points (Fig. 2c), which is frequently seen for Pluronics, including hyper-branched one [43]. The presence of the first break could be ascribed to the broad distribution of molecular weight of macromolecules, the change of molecular conformation at the air/water interface, or the formation of unimolecular micelles or oligomers [44,45]. Importantly, the surface tension of F127-CDs is lower than that of F127 within the whole investigated concentration range, and concentrations at the two inflection points (∼0.003 mg mL−1 and ∼0.53 mg mL−1) are smaller than those obtained from F127 (∼0.014 mg mL−1 and ∼6.75 mg mL−1). These results unambiguously indicated that both the efficacy and efficiency of the original surfactant (i.e., F127) have been improved after structural reconstruction through pyrolysis with CA. Consistent with this result, F127-CDs exhibit higher capability to disperse carbon nanotubes compared to F127 under the same experimental condition (Fig. S9).

The aggregation behavior of F127-CDs was explored by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements and imaging study. Below cmc, (∼0.53 mg mL−1), only unimers or small aggregates whose hydrodynamic radius are smaller than 4 nm (Fig. 2d). Above cmc, aggregates with sizes of 15–20 nm were detected. Cryo-TEM observation showed that the aggregates are spherical micelles (Fig. 2e). The aggregation behavior and the sizes of the micelles of F127-CDs are similar with those of F127 reported in the literature [46–48]. The similarity could be mainly ascribed to the long PEO-PPO-PEO chains attached to the carbonized core, as in case of short PEO chain, the aggregation behavior of the sa-CDs is much richer [27].

The structural change also leads to drastic changes in the adsorption-diffusion kinetic behavior of F127-CDs and F127 in water, as revealed by dynamic surface tension (DST) measurements (Fig. 2d). Rosen divided DST plot into four regions: (1) the induction region, (2) the rapid fall region, (3) the meso-equilibrium region, and (4) the equilibrium region. In Mathematics, most DST data could be fit by the following equation [49]:
(1)
γ 0
‐
γ t
γ t
‐
γ
e q
=
(
t
t ∗
)
n
where γ
0 is the surface tension of the pure solvent, γ
t is the surface tension of solution at time t, γ
eq is the equilibrium surface tension, t∗ is a constant with time units and n is a dimensionless constant.

Further, in order to quantitatively differentiate the different regions of DST, parameter t
i was defined as the end time of the induction region and t
m was defined as the beginning time of the meso-equilibrium region, then:
(2)
lg
t i
= lg
t ∗
‐
1 n
(3)
lg
t m
= lg
t ∗
+
1 n

According to this model, the rate at which F127-CDs reach the meso-equilibrium interval increased by two times. Interestingly, as branched or highly crosslinking surfactants, F127-CDs molecules require a long time to rearrange to obtain the correct adsorption orientation in the interfacial phase, since the small |dγ/dt| of F127-CDs compared with that of F127 in the meso-equilibrium region has been observed (Fig. 2d) [50]. The satisfactory surface activity of F127-CDs is an important basis for extending the application of surface/interfacial systems.

Being a nonionic surfactant, it is well-known that its surface activity is sensitive to temperature [48]. Considering that F127 occupies a large proportion in the structure of sa-CDs, temperature-dependent surface activity and PL property of F127-CDs were investigated. At elevated temperature, F127-CDs show higher capability to reduce the surface tension of water, characterized by both the decreased cmc and the surface tension above cmc (Fig. 3 a–c). The surface tension of an aqueous solution of 5 mg mL−1 F127-CDs is even lower than 30 mN m−1, indicating the potential application of F127-CDs in areas where high temperature is encountered. We believe this temperature-dependent phenomenon shares the same mechanism with F127, which undergoes dehydration when temperature rises. Zeta potential (ζ) measurement revealed that F127-CDs also carry a small amount of are negative charged with an average ζ of ∼ -5 mV (Fig. S10), which should originate from the remaining carboxy or hydroxy groups of the carbonized CA. Thus, besides owning characteristic properties of nonionic surfactant, theoretically F127-CDs also have properties of ionic surfactant, which is consistent with our recent discovery on TGME-CDs [27].

The PL intensity of F127-CDs decreased with increasing temperature (Fig. 3d and e). This behavior is commonly seen for CDs, which is attributed to the enhanced nonradiative relaxation of the molecular thermal motion [51]. DLS measurements on a 0.9 mg mL−1 aqueous solution of F127-CDs at different temperatures showed a continuous increase of the size of the aggregate (Fig. 3f), which is consistent with the temperature-dependent aggregation behavior of Pluronics [48]. From the linear correlation between the PL intensity and temperature (Fig. 3e), it can be concluded that the decrease of the photoluminescence of F127-CDs was mainly ascribed to the increase of temperature. Contribution from the enhanced aggregation, if there is any, can be neglected. Consistent with our explanation on the PL of F127-CDs in solvent-free state, the capability of F127-CDs to resist the ACQ effect with the aggregates should be ascribed to the PEO-PPO-PEO chain which well separate the carbonized cores.

Pluronics are a big class of block copolymers where the molecular structure can be varied by tuning the PEO/PPO ratio, the order of the blocks, and the length of the blocks [51]. Besides F127, another two Pluronics with different length of the central PPO part of different PPO/PEO ration (P123 and F68, see experimental section and the illustration in Fig. 4 a) were also selected for the preparation of sa-CDs under the same experimental condition as applied in F127-CDs. The strategy for the preparation of F127-CDs can be successfully applied to P123 and F68. Structural analysis showed that the signal attributed to the carbon core became more distinct in sa-CDs prepared by P123 and F68 which have shorter chains compared to F127 (Fig. 4b, Fig. S11). Zeta potential measurements showed that for an aqueous solution of 5 mg mL−1, a value of −7.32 mV was obtained for F68-CDs and −17.53 mV for P123-CDs (Fig. S12). In comparison, F127-CDs give a value of −6.15 mV at the same concentration. Considering that the negative charge mainly comes from the carbon nuclei formed after the pyrolysis of citric acid [52], the P123-CDs and F68-CDs possess larger proportions of carbonized cores compared to F127-CDs.

All the three CDs derived from Pluronics emission blue light. Variations in the structures of the Pluronics caused some difference in the PL properties of the CDs. Compared to F127-CDs, the λ ex at which the maximum PL intensity was recorded red shifted by 15 nm for F68-CDs, while blue shifted by 25 nm for P123-CDs (Fig. S13). The optimized concentration for PL changed from 0.9 mg mL−1 for F127-CDs to 2 mg mL−1 for F68-CDs and P123-CDs (Fig. S14). The maximum PL intensity follows the order of F127-CDs > F68-CDs > P123-CDs (Fig. 4c).

Obvious structure-property relationship was also reflected from surface activity. As seen from Fig. 4e and Fig. S15, F68-CDs exhibit lower surface tension than F68 within the investigated concentration range, which is consistent with the results from F127-CDs. For P123-CDs, its surface activity is comparable to P123. The variation rules of t∗ among different sa-CDs were found to be consistent with the corresponding Pluronics, and long PEO-PPO-PEO chains with high hydrophobicity reduce surface tension quickly. These observations confirmed that the amphiphilicity of sa-CDs is still mainly controlled by the Pluronic chains (Fig. 4f, Fig. S16). The surface activity parameters on Pluronic and Pluronic-derived sa-CDs have been summarized in Table S2.

Additionally, all kind of as-CDs can be prepared by this strategy with high yields. Taking the system of F127 as an example, 8.6 g CDs were obtained from 25 g raw materials (15 g of F127 and 10 g of CA), which gives a yield of 34.4 % calculated from the conversion of both F127 and CA, and ∼57.3 % if calculated solely from the conversion of F127. This is a considerably high yield in the research of CDs, not limited to sa-CDs, which is a big advantage. The success of the kilogram-scale mass production trials not only demonstrates (Fig. S17) that Pluronic-derived amphiphilic carbon dots are candidates for the next generation of luminescent surfactants, but also provided new insights to solve the problem [29,42,53,54] of lack of surface activity of CDs prepared from surfactant.

Being both luminescent and surface active, F127-CDs are ideal candidates for the construction of PL soft matter. Contains both hydrophilic and hydrophobic phases, ME has a variety of applications, such as daily chemicals, drug delivery, component slow-release, etc [55–57]. In addition, it is transparent, which enables deeper optical investigations. In current work, MEs were prepared following an established method with modification [58]. F127-CDs/P123 mixture was used as interfacial active species, and linalyl acetate (LAE, Fig. 5 a), which is a perfume, was selected as the oil phase. EtOH/H2O mixture (1:3 by volume) was used as the polar component. To expand the range of temperature at which the ME survives, a small amount of sodium deoxycholate (NaDC) was introduced (Figs. S18 and S19).

Influence of each component on the property of the PLME was investigated in detail. At a given composition of the ME with 2 v/v% LAE, 10 wt% P123 and 2 wt% NaDC, the PL intensity of the ME depends obviously with the content of F127-CDs, with the highest intensity occurring at 0.2 wt% (Fig. 3b–d). Increasing the content of LAE when other components are fixed leads to a continuous decrease of PL intensity (Fig. 3c–e). As the total area of the interface of the ME will increase with the content of LAE, the decrease of PL intensity could then be ascribed to the decreasing content of F127-CDs within the interfacial film. The maximum loading of LAE is 5 v/v%, above which F127-CDs/P123 mixture could only stabilize large droplets (in micrometers, Fig. S20) and the sample becomes turbid. The variation of PL intensity caused by the content of NaDC was also checked (Fig. S21), which is less pronounced compared to F127-CDs and LAE. The size of the ME droplets was monitored by cryo-SEM. Observations on four typical samples give sizes of droplets of 40–90 nm (Fig. 5f and g, Fig. S22). When LAE was replaced by four commercially available essential oils extracted from different plants, the MEs could still form, with slight modification on the emission color (Fig. S23). The universality of the ME formation with essential oils provides the opportunity for their formula modulation, which is useful in practical applications considering that the slower release of the oil phase in ME compared to bulk solution (Fig. S24).

Compared to organic dyes, one of the advantages of CDs is their capability toward photobleaching. The F127-CDs keeps this advantage in the ME, whose PL intensity shows negligible attenuation upon long time storage (up to three months, Fig. S25). The high PL stability, combined with the thermodynamic stability and high transparency, make the ME ideal candidate for deeper optical investigations. Specifically, materials exhibiting circularly polarized light (CPL) have received increasing attention in recent years [59,60]. Although research of CPL for crystals or polymer blends have been studied extensively, lese attention has been paid to soft materials in aqueous solutions [61–63]. In current formula of ME, we have a chiral component, i.e., NaDC. However, the ME containing 2 % NaDC only exhibited a weak circular dichroism (CD) signal before 240 nm (Fig. S26). To enhance the chirality of the ME, phenylglycine alcohol (PGA), a chiral compound (Fig. S27) that is soluble in EtOH solution of P123 but slightly soluble in water and LAE (Fig. S28), was added. As shown in Fig. 6 a, CD signals between 230 nm and 275 nm were detected whose intensity increases with increasing amount of PGA, and good Cotton effect was noticed with L- and D-PGA. CPL signals of the ME were successfully obtained (Fig. 6b). At a concentration of PGA of 4 mg mL−1, the sample with D-PGA gives an asymmetric factor (glum) of 1.72 × 10−3, and that with L-PGA has a value of −2.23 × 10−3.

It should be noted that the selection of the chiral components plays a key role on the successful creation of the CPL signal. The molecular structure of PGA (see Fig. 5a) has a good hydrophobic/hydrophilic balance which integrates it into the interfacial film containing F127-CDs. If PGA was replaced by (R)-2-phenyl-1-propanol (R–PP), no significant chiral transfer and CPL could be found (Figs. S29 and S30). We have also checked another type of blue-emitting CDs which are lack of surface activity, no obvious CPL could be found even with PGA (Fig. S31). Thus, the integration of the PL and chiral components in the interfacial film plays a key role for the successful creation of the CPL material. The rule extracted here is helpful for the design of new CPL-active soft materials.

In summary, we have shown that the strategy used to produce CDs is effective for deep processing of Pluronics. Compared to CDs obtained by pyrolysis of other organic molecules, the CDs derived from Pluronics are surface active and could be obtained in large scale. In case of F127, the surface activity of the CDs is even higher after deep processing. Being both photoluminescent and surface active, the CDs were applied to construct luminescent soft materials using microemulsion as a typical example. By further introducing chiral molecules to the system, circularly polarized luminescence was achieved. While the controlled release of the liquid flavor or essential oil in the microemulsion provides opportunities of formula modulation towards applications in food and cosmetics, the emergence of CPL opens the door for applications in photonic devices.

Ning Feng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Shulin Li: Resources, Formal analysis. Xiaoqian Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaoli Ji: Methodology, Investigation. Lei Feng: Supervision, Project administration. Hongguang Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875129), and the assistance of Center for Structural and Property Analysis, Core Facilities Sharing Platform, Shandong University.
